THE STANDARD FOR AWARDING FEES UNDER GOVERNMENT 
CODE SECTION 91012 AND 42 U.S.C. SECTION 1988 IS

The standard for awarding attorney's fees under Section

neither sets forth standards for the exercise of the discretion. 
In addition, the purpose for permitting attorney's fees i 
basically the same in both statutes.

The leading case used to interpret 42 U.S.C. Section 1988 
is Newman v. Piggy Park Enterprises, Inc. (1968) 390 U.S. 400, 
401- 4 03, 88 S. Ct. 964. 
(See Hensley v. Eckerhart (May 16, 
.) 
The case itself predates the 
1983) 2.1 
U.S. 
enactment of 42 U.S.C. Section 1988 by many years. 
However, since 
Newman interpreted 42 U.S.C. Sections 2000a-3(a), which like 42 
U.S.C. Section 1988, provides that a "prevailing party" in a 
desegregation case is entitled to "a reasonable attorney's fee" in 
the court's "discretion," the courts have uniformly applied it to 
cases arising under Section 1988. 
In Newman, the Supreme Court stated that: 
When the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was passed, 
it was evident that enforcement would prove diffi-
cult and that the Nation would have to rely in 
part upon private litigation as a means of 
securing broad compliance with the law. 
A Title 
II suit is thus private in form only. 
When a plaintiff brings an action under that 
Title, he cannot recover damages. 
If he obtains 
an injunction, he does so not for himself alone 
but also as a "private attorney general," vindi-
cating a policy that Congress considered of the 
highest priority. 
If successful plaintiffs were 
routinely forced to bear their own attorneys' 
fees, few aggrieved parties would be in a position 
to advance the public interest by invoking the 
injunctive powers of the federal courts. 
Congress 
therefore enacted the provision for counsel fees 
-- not simply to penalize litigants who 
~I As with Section 91012 of the Political Reform Act, 
Section 54960.5 grants the trial court discretion to award 
attorney's fees; it encourages private enforcement of public 
policy; and it does not set forth express criteria governing the 
award of fees.

embedding.summary: This is a respondent's opening brief filed by the Fair Political Practices Commission in a California Court of Appeal case (No. 4 Civil 28673) regarding whether the FPPC is liable for attorney's fees incurred by plaintiffs in a challenge to campaign contribution disclosure requirements. The brief argues the FPPC should not be liable for fees incurred before its intervention and addresses statutory authority and laches issues.
